MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION ; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Feb-27-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances, not contested, and the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant McCord Corporation's Motion for Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Defendant fails to show there is a difference in fact, law, or circumstance to necessitate review of its renewed motion under C.C.P. section 1008.

The Court made no error in denying Defendant's prior motion. The last day of service of the notice of motion is determined by the last day of hearing (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (a)(3)), the last day of the 75-day notice period (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (a)(2)), and the addition of court days to the notice period depending on the method of delivery (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (a)(2)). The calculation is counted backwards if the last day falls on a holiday (Code Civ. Proc., secs. 12, 12a, and 12c; Code Civ. Proc., sec. 135 and Gov. Code, sec. 6700). Saturdays and Sundays are holidays, making Friday, June 9, 2023 the last day of the 75-day notice period. With the addition of two court days under C.C.P. section 437c(a)(2), the last day of service was Wednesday, June 7, 2023. Defendant filed the notice by electronic service on Thursday, June 8, 2023. Defendant's notice of motion was untimely.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Bailiff: Not Present. Not Reported. (301/RCE)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice Of Motion In Support Of Its Motion For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Aug-24-2023 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances, not contested, and tentative ruling adopted as follows: Defendant McCord Corporation's Motion for Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Objection filed by Plaintiff on the ground of insufficient notice.

A motion for summary adjudication must be heard no later than 30 days before the date of trial. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (a)(3).) Since the trial date in this action is Monday, September 25, 2023, the last date for a summary adjudication hearing is Saturday, August 26, 2023. Saturdays are court holidays. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 135; see also Gov. Code, sec. 6700.) Accordingly, the last day for a summary adjudication hearing is Friday, August 25, 2023. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 12; Code Civ. Proc., sec. 12a, subd. (a); Code Civ. Proc., sec. 12c.)

The notice of motion must be served on all parties at least 75 days before the date of hearing. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (a)(2).) Defendant needed to serve its notice of motion by Saturday, June 10, 2023. Saturdays are treated as court holidays. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 135; see also Gov. Code, sec. 6700.) Accordingly, the last day for service was Friday, June 9, 2023. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 12; Code Civ. Proc., sec. 12a, subd. (a); Code Civ. Proc. sec. 12c.)

If the notice of motion is served by a method of delivery providing for overnight delivery, the 75-day period of notice must be increased by two court days. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c(a)(2).) Electronic service and filing constitute such a method of delivery. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 1010.6, subd. (a).) Accordingly, the last day for service was Wednesday, June 7, 2023. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 12c, subd. (b).)

Defendant's proof of service reflects that Defendant delivered its notice of motion by electronic service and filing on Thursday, June 8, 2023. The last day for such method of delivery was Wednesday, June 7, 2023. Defendant's notice of motion is therefore untimely. Order signed. Judge: Samuel K. Feng, Clerk: J. Alameda. Not reported. (503/SKF)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Aug-24-2023 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via CourtCall:
Andrew Chew of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiff;

Paul Lannus of LANKFORD CRAWFORD MORENO & OSTERTAG (925-300-3520), appearing for Defendant Ford Motor Company.

RULING:
Contested, argued, and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Harvey M. Harper Co.'s Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant.

The Court took judicial notice of the following exhibits in Defendant's Index of Evidence in Support of the Motion: Exhibit A (Decedent's Personal Injury Complaint); Exhibit B (Plaintiffs' Wrongful Death Complaint); and Exhibit C (Amendment to First Amended Wrongful Death Complaint to Substitute Defendant's True Name for Does 2 and 254). The Court considered Exhibits D through K in Defendant's Index of Evidence in Support of the Motion.

The Court declines to rule on Defendant's objections to Decedent's deposition submitted in Plaintiffs' opposing evidence. The objections are not material to its disposition of the motion. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (q).)

Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence that Decedent was directly or indirectly exposed to asbestos from asbestos-containing products supplied by Defendant. (See Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.)

"[T]he placement and quantum of the burden of proof at trial [are] crucial for purposes of summary judgment." (Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 851.) As the proponents of Decedent's deposition, Plaintiffs have the burden of proof at trial that the deposition testimony is, more likely than not, admissible under the former testimony hearsay exception in Evidence Code, section 1291, subdivision (a). (See Berroteran v. Superior Court (2022) 12 Cal.5th 867, 895; see also Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 851.)

To meet its initial burden on summary judgment, Defendant must therefore present evidence that would require a reasonable trier of fact not to find any underlying material fact of the deposition's admissibility more likely than not. (See Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 851.) Defendant may, for example, "present evidence that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence [regarding the admissibility of deposition testimony]-as through admissions by the plaintiff following extensive discovery to the effect that he has discovered nothing." (Id. at p. 855.)

Plaintiffs disclosed Decedent's deposition testimony in their responses to Defendant's special interrogatories responses as one of their documents relating to Decedent's exposures to asbestos. (See Defendant's Index of Evidence in Support of the Motion, Ex. E [Plaintiffs' Responses to Defendant's Special Interrogatories, Set One] at 8:21-22, 9:1-3.) Defendant fails to set forth sufficient facts with evidentiary support in its separate statement of undisputed material facts regarding the admissibility of Decedent's deposition. (See Opp. at 10:15-16.) Defendant instead offers only argument in its moving papers that "[w]hile decedent was deposed, Harper Motors was not present at the deposition and her testimony is therefore not admissible against Harper Motors." (Motion at 7:6-8.)

Though the deposition's admissibility is an issue to be decided by the trial judge, Defendant fails to show that a reasonable trier of fact would not find any underlying material fact of its admissibility more likely than not. (See Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 851.) As such, Defendant has not shown that Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence of product identification in this wrongful death action. Order signed. Judge: Samuel K. Feng, Clerk: J. Alameda, Court Reporter: Jaime Ply, CSR #13323 (via CourtCall). (503/SKF)
